notes: IBM launched Network Station September 1996. Renamed IBM NetVista April 2000. Withdrawn April 2002 with no replacement. Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IBM_NetVista
Announced May 20, 1996. Defined minimum specifications for Network Computers. Standard never achieved wide adoption.
Sun JavaStation announced October 1996; sub-$750 diskless Java thin client. Discontinued as part of Sun's decline.

lifecycle_current: Active but server-dominant (Java on desktop failed; server Java thrives)
notes: Aberdeen saw strong ISV Java adoption for NCs. Desktop Java failed; server-side Java (JVM, Spring, Jakarta EE) became dominant.

lifecycle_current: Niche/Embedded (processor line continued in embedded; PPC for desktop ended)
notes: Network Station used PowerPC 403 with additional Java processors. PPC 403 line continued in embedded/automotive; desktop PPC ended when Apple switched to Intel (2006).

lifecycle_current: Discontinued (superseded by Citrix MetaFrame / Windows Terminal Services)
notes: NCD WinCenter Pro enabled server-hosted Windows NT applications for thin clients. Superseded by Citrix and Windows Terminal Services.

lifecycle_current: Discontinued (replaced by more modern endpoint management)
notes: Java/HTML-based management tool for centralized Network Station configuration. Discontinued with product line 2002.

lifecycle_current: Active and growing (dominant thin client paradigm in education)
notes: The NC concept's vindication. Chromebooks achieve the low-cost, centrally-managed, browser/app-centric model Aberdeen described for NCs. Google sold 40M+ Chromebooks/year at peak.

lifecycle_current: Active and growing (Windows 365 Cloud PC; Amazon WorkSpaces; Citrix)
notes: The enterprise NC vision materialized as VDI/DaaS (Desktop as a Service). The cost and management arguments Aberdeen made in 1997 are the core VDI/cloud PC value proposition today.
